attributes.stem: An otherwise well 45-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of left foot pain and discomfort. She complains of a burning sensation in the base of her foot, specifically between the 3rd and 4th metatarsals, and the sensation radiates distally towards the 3rd and 4th digits. She wears high heels to work every day and also runs recreationally on the weekends. On examination, she is systemically well and there is no neurovascular compromise of her affected limb, with completely intact skin. Pain can be elicited on palpation at the metatarsal heads and a click is heard. Tapping on the same area causes shooting pain down into the 3rd and 4th digits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Intermetatarsal neuroma, or Morton neuroma, is a benign tumour caused by entrapment or enlargement of one of the plantar digital nerves. In this case, it is likely to be a branch of the medial plantar nerve that is compressed due to chronic high-heeled footwear use, which typically presents with neuropathic pain in the plantar surface of the foot. On examination, Mulder's sign may be positive, which refers to the pain elicited when pressure is applied on the plantar surface of the foot, producing an audible click known as Mulder's click. Due to the nerve compression, Tinel sign can also be elicited on tapping of the metatarsal heads, resulting in shooting pain radiating distally.

Intermetatarsal neuromas are diagnosed clinically but can be confirmed via ultrasound or MRI. Initial treatment includes conservative measures such as reducing pressure on the metatarsal heads with a change of footwear or using orthotics. Failing which, corticosteroid and local anaesthetic injections can be considered. Surgical resection may be necessary for refractory cases, where a trial of non-surgical management for 9-12 months fails to provide symptomatic relief.

3rd metatarsal stress fractures are common in people who have high-intensity training programs that involve running long distances or carrying heavy loads, such as military personnel or marathoners. They usually present with acute pain which worsens with activity and is relieved with rest, and can be associated with localised swelling. In this case, the pain has been going on for 2 months and is more likely to be neuropathic due to the burning/tingling sensation, with a positive Tinel sign, which does not correlate with the clinical presentation of a stress fracture.

1st metatarsophalangeal joint sprain (Turf toe) is a ligamentous injury commonly caused by hyperextension of the big toe, and is usually associated with sports that involve change in directions or sudden stops, such as sprinting of football. It is characterised by acute pain and swelling localised to the 1st metatarsophalangeal joint, and also limited range of motion of the first toe. It does not fit the background and clinical presentation of this case, with pain localised to the metatarsal heads on palpation.

Osteomyelitis is an infection of the bone and its surrounding structures. It can result from contiguous spread of infection from adjacent structures, direct inoculation such as in open fractures or from haematogenous spread with a bacteraemic event. It usually presents with localised pain together with systemic features such as a fever, fatigue, and chills, and can sometimes cause the formation of an abscess if untreated. Based on the chronicity and stability of the patient's symptoms, lack of systemic features and intact skin, osteomyelitis is less likely. Additionally, a positive Mulder's and Tinel's sign would also not be expected in osteomyelitis.

Plantar fasciitis is the inflammation of the plantar fascia, a thick band of tissue running along the bottom of the foot. It most commonly affects runners and people who spend a lot of time on their feet, which is consistent with this patient's background. However, the pain in plantar fasciitis is usually sharp in nature, worse in the mornings with the initial steps or after prolonged rest but improves after a few minutes of walking, and localised to the heel (calcaneus) or arch of the foot (metatarsal base). It also does not cause usually cause neuropathic pain or Tinel's sign, which points more towards a nerve pathology.
learningPoints: Mulder sign - Pain on applying pressure to plantar surface of foot at the metatarsal heads, which can also elicit an audible click (Mulder's click).
Tinel sign - Numbness/tingling/weakness elicited in the distribution of the nerve by lightly tapping on the nerve suspected to be compressed/injured
references: Matthews BG, Hurn SE, Harding MP, Henry RA, Ware RS. The effectiveness of non-surgical interventions for common plantar digital compressive neuropathy (Morton's neuroma): a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Foot Ankle Res. 2019 Feb 13;12:12. doi: 10.1186/s13047-019-0320-7. PMID: 30809275; PMCID: PMC6375221. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30809275/

Bhatia M, Thomson L. Morton's neuroma - Current concepts review. J Clin Orthop Trauma. 2020 May-Jun;11(3):406-409. doi: 10.1016/j.jcot.2020.03.024. Epub 2020 Apr 10. PMID: 32405199; PMCID: PMC7211826. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7211826/

Munir U, Tafti D, Morgan S. Morton Neuroma. 2023 May 22. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan–. PMID: 29262171. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29262171/
